rgb2hsv.description:
```glsl
vec3 rgb2hsv(vec3 c)
```

Convert sRGB to HSV. PERF: CHEAP — Sam Hocevar branchless trick.

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/rgb2hsv.glsl`

hsv2rgb.description:
```glsl
vec3 hsv2rgb(vec3 c)
```

Convert HSV to sRGB. PERF: CHEAP — branchless.

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/hsv2rgb.glsl`

palette.description:
```glsl
vec3 palette(float t, vec3 a, vec3 b, vec3 c, vec3 d)
```

Inigo Quilez's cosine palette. `a` = bias, `b` = amplitude, `c` = frequency, `d` = phase. PERF: FREE — one cos per channel.

**Example:**
```glsl
vec3 col = palette(uv.x,
  vec3(0.5), vec3(0.5),
  vec3(1.0), vec3(0.0, 0.33, 0.67));
```

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/palette.glsl`

tonemapACES.description:
```glsl
vec3 tonemapACES(vec3 col [, float exposure])
```

Narkowicz's fast ACES filmic curve. Inputs in linear HDR, outputs in [0,1]. Default exposure 0.6 is the sweet spot for typical lit scenes.
PERF: CHEAP — one rational polynomial.

**Example:**
```glsl
col = tonemapACES(col);          // default exposure 0.6
col = tonemapACES(col, 0.4);      // dimmer
```

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/tonemap.glsl`

multiplyBlend.description:
```glsl
vec3 multiplyBlend(vec3 base, vec3 blend)
```

Photoshop-style multiply. Darkens — use for shadow layers, dirt passes, tinted overlays.
PERF: FREE — one component-wise multiply.

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/blend.glsl`

screenBlend.description:
```glsl
vec3 screenBlend(vec3 base, vec3 blend)
```

Photoshop-style screen blend. Brightens — the mathematical inverse of multiply.

Use for additive light effects (sun haze, glow halos, lens flare, fire) where straight `base + blend` would blow out, but you still want the blend to lift the base.
PERF: FREE.

**Example:**
```glsl
col = screenBlend(col, glowColor * glow * 0.8);
```

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/blend.glsl`

overlayBlend.description:
```glsl
vec3 overlayBlend(vec3 base, vec3 blend)
```

Multiply where base is dark, screen where base is bright. Increases contrast while preserving midtones — good for grading passes.
PERF: CHEAP.

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/blend.glsl`

softLightBlend.description:
```glsl
vec3 softLightBlend(vec3 base, vec3 blend)
```

Gentler than overlay — common in photo color grading.
PERF: CHEAP (one sqrt per channel).

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/blend.glsl`

addBlend.description:
```glsl
vec3 addBlend(vec3 base, vec3 blend)
```

Linear dodge — straight addition. Can clip to white, by design.
Use for: hot glow cores, fire centers, lens-flare hot spots, explosion peaks.
PERF: FREE.

**Example:**
```glsl
col = addBlend(col, vec3(1.0, 0.7, 0.3) * sparkMask);
```

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/blend.glsl`

darkenBlend.description:
```glsl
vec3 darkenBlend(vec3 base, vec3 blend)
```

Per-channel min. Keeps the darker of the two layers.
Use for: shadow compositing, masking by intersection, vignette stacking.
PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/blend.glsl`

lightenBlend.description:
```glsl
vec3 lightenBlend(vec3 base, vec3 blend)
```

Per-channel max. Keeps the brighter of the two layers.
Use for: fire / sparks / bright highlights composited over a base — the bright layer shows through without dimming areas where the base is already brighter.
PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/blend.glsl`

differenceBlend.description:
```glsl
vec3 differenceBlend(vec3 base, vec3 blend)
```

Absolute difference per channel. Symmetric, XOR-like.
Use for: motion graphics transitions, color-cycle effects, hot/cold heat maps, any "show where two signals diverge" visualization.
PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/blend.glsl`

colorDodgeBlend.description:
```glsl
vec3 colorDodgeBlend(vec3 base, vec3 blend)
```

Extreme brightening — base / (1 - blend). As `blend` approaches 1, output blows out to white very fast. Strongest "intense light" effect.
Use for: lens flares, magic sparkles, glints, intense highlights.
Guarded with epsilon so the 1/0 case stays finite.
PERF: CHEAP (3 divides).

*Auto-imports* `/core/color/blend.glsl`

clamp01.description:
```glsl
T clamp01(T x) — clamp to [0, 1]
```

PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/clamp.glsl`

saturate.description:
```glsl
float|vec3 saturate(T x) — clamp to [0, 1] (HLSL alias)
```

HLSL/Unity-style alias for `clamp(x, 0.0, 1.0)`. PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/saturate.glsl`

lerp.description:
```glsl
T lerp(T a, T b, float t) — HLSL alias for mix
```

PERF: FREE — delegates to `mix`.

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/lerp.glsl`

min3.description:
```glsl
float min3(vec3 v) — minimum of three components
```

PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/minmax.glsl`

max3.description:
```glsl
float max3(vec3 v) — maximum of three components
```

PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/minmax.glsl`

remap.description:
```glsl
float remap(value, inMin, inMax, outMin, outMax)
```

Linear remap from one range to another.
PERF: FREE.

**Example:**
```glsl
float x = remap(u_time, 0.0, 10.0, -1.0, 1.0);  // time → [-1, 1]
```

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/remap.glsl`

remap01.description:
```glsl
float remap01(value, inMin, inMax) — remap into [0, 1], clamped
```

Remaps to [0,1] with hard clamp at edges. PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/remap.glsl`

fit.description:
```glsl
float fit(x, a, b, c, d) — alias for remap
```

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/range.glsl`

fit01.description:
```glsl
float fit01(x, a, b) — alias for remap01
```

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/range.glsl`

signNoZero.description:
```glsl
float signNoZero(float x) — sign that never returns 0
```

Like `sign()` but +1 for x>=0. Useful when you need a hard +/-1 direction.
PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/sign.glsl`

smoothstep01.description:
```glsl
float smoothstep01(float x) — smoothstep(0, 1, x)
```

Convenience for the common smoothstep(0, 1, x) case.
PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/smoothstep.glsl`

stepIn.description:
```glsl
float stepIn(float edge, float x) — step rising at edge
```

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/step.glsl`

stepOut.description:
```glsl
float stepOut(float edge, float x) — step falling at edge
```

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/step.glsl`

rot2.description:
```glsl
mat2 rot2(float a) — CCW rotation matrix
```

PERF: FREE — one cos + one sin + matrix build.

*Auto-imports* `/core/math/math.glsl`

rotate.description:
```glsl
vec2|vec3 rotate(p, angle [, center | axes])
```

2D rotation around center (or origin); 3D combined XYZ rotation via an angles vec3.
PERF: FREE — sin/cos + small matrix product.

*Auto-imports* `/core/transform/rotate.glsl`

rotateX.description:
```glsl
vec3 rotateX(vec3 p, float angle)
```

Rotate around the X axis. PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/transform/rotate.glsl`

rotateY.description:
```glsl
vec3 rotateY(vec3 p, float angle)
```

Rotate around the Y axis. PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/transform/rotate.glsl`

rotateZ.description:
```glsl
vec3 rotateZ(vec3 p, float angle)
```

Rotate around the Z axis. PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/transform/rotate.glsl`

scale.description:
```glsl
vec2|vec3 scale(p, factor [, center])
```

Inverse scale (divides by factor) so SDF distances stay correct.
PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/transform/scale.glsl`

translate.description:
```glsl
vec2|vec3 translate(p, offset) — subtract offset
```

PERF: FREE — one subtract.

*Auto-imports* `/core/transform/translate.glsl`

smootherstep.description:
```glsl
float smootherstep(float edge0, float edge1, float x)
```

Perlin quintic: `6t⁵ − 15t⁴ + 10t³`. Smoother than the cubic `smoothstep` at edges — both first AND second derivative are zero at endpoints. Use for noise interpolation and animation curves where the cubic discontinuity shows.
PERF: CHEAP — same family as smoothstep, ~2 extra muls.

*Auto-imports* `/core/util/smoothstep2.glsl`

invSmoothstep.description:
```glsl
float invSmoothstep(float x) — inverse of smoothstep(0,1,x)
```

Maps a smoothstep output back to its input. Useful when chaining ease curves.
PERF: CHEAP — one asin/sin.

*Auto-imports* `/core/util/smoothstep2.glsl`

stepmap.description:
```glsl
T stepmap(T edge, T value, T outMin, T outMax)
```

Hard step then linear remap to a target range. Like `mix(outMin, outMax, step(edge, value))` but in one call.
PERF: FREE.

*Auto-imports* `/core/util/stepmap.glsl`

hash11.description:
```glsl
float hash11(float p) — 1D → 1D, output in [0, 1]
```

Hoskins hash. Hashes a float to a float in [0, 1]. PERF: CHEAP — branchless mul/fract.

*Auto-imports* `/noise/hash.glsl`

hash12.description:
```glsl
float hash12(vec2 p) — 2D → 1D
```

Hashes a vec2 to a float in [0, 1]. Use for grid-cell random values, dithering, grain.
PERF: CHEAP.

**Example:**
```glsl
float r = hash12(floor(uv * 10.0));  // random value per 10×10 grid cell
```

*Auto-imports* `/noise/hash.glsl`

hash13.description:
```glsl
float hash13(vec3 p) — 3D → 1D
```

Hashes a vec3 to a float in [0, 1]. For 3D voxel grids.
PERF: CHEAP.

*Auto-imports* `/noise/hash.glsl`

hash21.description:
```glsl
vec2 hash21(float p) — 1D → 2D
```

Hashes a float to a vec2 (each component in [0, 1]).
PERF: CHEAP.

*Auto-imports* `/noise/hash.glsl`

hash22.description:
```glsl
vec2 hash22(vec2 p) — 2D → 2D
```

Hashes a vec2 to a vec2. Commonly used for grid-cell offset generation in Worley/cellular noise.
PERF: CHEAP.

*Auto-imports* `/noise/hash.glsl`

hash33.description:
```glsl
vec3 hash33(vec3 p) — 3D → 3D
```

Hashes a vec3 to a vec3. Used for 3D random direction vectors (gradient noise, particle dispersal).
PERF: CHEAP.

*Auto-imports* `/noise/hash.glsl`

valueNoise2D.description:
```glsl
float valueNoise2D(vec2 p) — output in [0, 1]
```

Value noise: hashes 4 corner values of the unit cell containing p, smoothly interpolates with a Hermite curve.
Look: soft, "blurry", organic. Cheapest of the smooth noises.
PERF: CHEAP — 4 hashes + bilinear.
For sharper detail use simplexNoise2D; for cellular look use worleyNoise2D.

**Example:**
```glsl
float n = valueNoise2D(uv * 5.0);  // 5 cells across the screen
```

*Auto-imports* `/noise/value.glsl`

valueNoise3D.description:
```glsl
float valueNoise3D(vec3 p) — output in [0, 1]
```

Value noise in 3D — 8 corner hashes + trilinear blend. PERF: CHEAP — 8 hashes + trilinear.
2x cost of valueNoise2D.

*Auto-imports* `/noise/value.glsl`

simplexNoise2D.description:
```glsl
float simplexNoise2D(vec2 p) — output in [-1, 1]
```

Simplex noise (Ken Perlin's 2001 update of Perlin noise). Uses tetrahedral simplices instead of cubic cells — no axis-aligned banding artifacts. Output range [-1, 1] (zero mean).
PERF: MODERATE — ~5× valueNoise2D cost. Worth it for sharp detail.
Use for: terrain, organic patterns, sharper FBM than value-based.

**Example:**
```glsl
float n = simplexNoise2D(uv * 3.0 + u_time * 0.2);  // animated drift
```

*Auto-imports* `/noise/simplex.glsl`

simplexNoise3D.description:
```glsl
float simplexNoise3D(vec3 p) — output in [-1, 1]
```

Simplex noise in 3D. Significantly faster than 3D Perlin (5 tetrahedra vs 8 cubes).
PERF: MODERATE.
Use for: volumetric noise, 3D terrain, time-as-Z animated 2D patterns.

*Auto-imports* `/noise/simplex.glsl`

perlinNoise2D.description:
```glsl
float perlinNoise2D(vec2 p) — output in roughly [-1, 1]
```

Classic Perlin gradient noise (1983). Axis-aligned cubic grid, dot-product gradients at corners. Subtly different "feel" from simplex — slightly more axis bias.
PERF: MODERATE — similar cost to simplex in 2D.
In most cases prefer `simplexNoise2D` (faster in 3D + no axis artifacts).

*Auto-imports* `/noise/perlin.glsl`

perlinNoise3D.description:
```glsl
float perlinNoise3D(vec3 p) — output in roughly [-1, 1]
```

Perlin gradient noise in 3D. PERF: MODERATE. Prefer simplexNoise3D unless you specifically want the cubic-grid look.

*Auto-imports* `/noise/perlin.glsl`

worleyNoise2D.description:
```glsl
vec2 worleyNoise2D(vec2 p) — returns (F1, F2): nearest and 2nd-nearest distances
```

Cellular noise (Steven Worley, 1996). Returns the distance to the nearest feature point (F1) and the 2nd-nearest (F2). Many effects use combinations:
- F1: classic cellular pattern (rocks, scales, cells)
- F2 − F1: sharp edges between cells (great for cracked-mud, stained-glass)
- F1 × F2: pebble-like
PERF: MODERATE — examines 9 neighboring cells (2D) for accurate F1/F2.

**Example:**
```glsl
vec2 wf = worleyNoise2D(uv * 8.0);
float edges = smoothstep(0.0, 0.05, wf.y - wf.x);  // crack pattern
```

*Auto-imports* `/noise/worley.glsl`

worleyNoise3D.description:
```glsl
vec2 worleyNoise3D(vec3 p) — (F1, F2) for volumetric cellular
```

Cellular noise in 3D. Examines 27 neighbor cells.
PERF: EXPENSIVE — significantly more cost than 2D variants. Use sparingly in raymarch loops.

*Auto-imports* `/noise/worley.glsl`

fbm.description:
```glsl
float fbm(vec2|vec3 p, int octaves [, float lacunarity, float gain])
```

Fractal Brownian Motion — sums multiple octaves of **simplex noise** at increasing frequencies and decreasing amplitudes. The workhorse for natural-looking textures.

- `octaves` — number of layers (typical 3–8). Cost scales linearly.
- `lacunarity` — frequency multiplier per octave (default 2.0 = each octave is double frequency).
- `gain` — amplitude multiplier per octave (default 0.5 = each octave half as strong).

Output is approximately in [-1, 1].

PERF: MODERATE × octaves — each octave is one simplex evaluation. 5-octave fbm = ~5× simplex cost.

Use for: sharp/detailed natural patterns (terrain, marble, fire).
For cloudier/softer look use `fbmSoft` (value-noise based).

**Example:**
```glsl
float t = u_time * 0.2;
vec2  q = uv * 3.0 + vec2(t, -t);
float n = fbm(q, 5);  // 5 octaves, default lacunarity/gain
```

*Auto-imports* `/noise/fbm.glsl`

fbmSoft.description:
```glsl
float fbmSoft(vec2 p, int octaves [, float lacunarity, float gain])
```

Like `fbm` but uses **value noise** instead of simplex. Output is softer and cloudier — better for organic blob shapes (clouds, mist, smoke) than for sharp detail.
PERF: CHEAPER than `fbm` — value noise is ~5× cheaper than simplex per octave.
Use when you want a "puffy" look or to optimize a heavy fbm chain.

**Example:**
```glsl
float n = fbmSoft(uv + warp, 5, 2.5, 0.5);  // wider lacunarity for fluffier output
```

*Auto-imports* `/noise/fbm.glsl`

turbulence.description:
```glsl
float turbulence(vec2|vec3 p, int octaves [, float lacunarity, float gain])
```

Turbulence — sums octaves of **|simplexNoise|**. The abs() fold creates sharp creases at zero-crossings of the underlying noise; stacking them builds "billowy" structure.

Different visual primitive from `fbm` — not derivable cheaply at the call site because `abs()` must happen *inside* the octave loop.

Output range: ~[0, 1] (strictly non-negative, zero mean).

PERF: MODERATE × octaves — same cost as `fbm` (`abs()` is free).

Use for: fire, smoke wisps, marble veins, water caustics, cloth folds.

**Example:**
```glsl
float t = u_time * 0.3;
float n = turbulence(uv * 4.0 + vec2(0.0, t), 5);
vec3 col = mix(vec3(0.1, 0.0, 0.0), vec3(1.0, 0.7, 0.2), n);  // fire
```

*Auto-imports* `/noise/turbulence.glsl`

ridged.description:
```glsl
float ridged(vec2|vec3 p, int octaves [, float sharpness=2.0])
```

Ridged multifractal — sums octaves of `(1 - |simplexNoise|)^sharpness`. Sharp peaks form at zero-crossings of the underlying noise, then `pow` pinches them into ridges.

**sharpness guide:**
- `1.0` → soft, rolling ridges (almost-fbm)
- `2.0` → standard mountain ridges (default — uses cheap `n*n` instead of `pow`)
- `4.0+` → razor-sharp veins, lightning

Output range: ~[0, 1].
PERF: MODERATE × octaves. Default 2.0 path avoids `pow` for speed.

Use for: mountain ridges, lightning veins, cracked terrain, alien skin patterns.

**Example:**
```glsl
float h = ridged(uv * 2.0, 6);          // default sharpness = 2.0
float h2 = ridged(uv * 2.0, 6, 4.0);     // sharper, lightning-like
```

*Auto-imports* `/noise/ridged.glsl`

sdCircle.description:
```glsl
float sdCircle(vec2 p, float r)
```

Signed distance to a circle of radius `r` centered at the origin.

- **< 0** → inside the disk
- **= 0** → on the boundary
- **> 0** → outside

The simplest 2D SDF. Translate by offsetting `p` before the call.

**Example:**
```glsl
vec2 uv = (gl_FragCoord.xy * 2.0 - u_resolution) / min(u_resolution.x, u_resolution.y);
float d = sdCircle(uv - vec2(0.2, 0.0), 0.3);
vec3 col = vec3(smoothstep(0.005, 0.0, d));
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/2d/circle.glsl`

sdBox.description:
```glsl
float sdBox(vec2|vec3 p, vec2|vec3 b) — half-extents
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/2d/box.glsl, /sdf/3d/box.glsl`

sdSegment.description:
```glsl
float sdSegment(vec2 p, vec2 a, vec2 b)
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/2d/segment.glsl`

sdRoundedBox.description:
```glsl
float sdRoundedBox(vec2|vec3 p, vec2|vec3 b, float r)
```

Box with rounded corners. PERF: FREE — same cost as sdBox + one subtract.

For per-corner radii (2D only), use `sdRoundedBox4(p, b, vec4(tr, br, tl, bl))`.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = sdRoundedBox(uv, vec2(0.4, 0.25), 0.08);
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/2d/rounded_box.glsl, /sdf/3d/rounded_box.glsl`

sdRoundedBox4.description:
```glsl
float sdRoundedBox4(vec2 p, vec2 b, vec4 r) — per-corner radii (tr, br, tl, bl)
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/2d/rounded_box.glsl`

sdTriangle.description:
```glsl
float sdTriangle(vec2 p, vec2 a, vec2 b, vec2 c)
```

Triangle from three vertices. PERF: MODERATE — three per-edge clamps + sqrt. Use sparingly; for many triangles, batch via a polygon SDF.

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/2d/triangle.glsl`

sdEquilateralTriangle.description:
```glsl
float sdEquilateralTriangle(vec2 p, float r) — pointing up, circumradius r
```

Equilateral triangle. PERF: FREE — much cheaper than the general sdTriangle.

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/2d/triangle.glsl`

sdTorus.description:
```glsl
float sdTorus(vec3 p, vec2 t) — (major, minor) radii
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/torus.glsl`

sdCapsule.description:
```glsl
float sdCapsule(vec3 p, vec3 a, vec3 b, float r)
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/capsule.glsl`

sdPlane.description:
```glsl
float sdPlane(vec3 p, vec3 n, float h) — n normalized
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/plane.glsl`

sdCylinder.description:
```glsl
float sdCylinder(vec3 p, float h, float r) — Y-axis, h=half-height
```

Capped cylinder along Y axis. PERF: FREE — cheaper than sdCapsule.

There is also a 4-arg overload `sdCylinder(p, vec3 a, vec3 b, float r)` for arbitrary-axis cylinders.

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/cylinder.glsl`

sdOctahedron.description:
```glsl
float sdOctahedron(vec3 p, float s)
```

Octahedron. PERF: FREE — L1-norm approximation, very fast.
For a true Lipschitz=1 SDF (matters for thin detail / accurate shadows) use `sdOctahedronExact`.

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/octahedron.glsl`

sdOctahedronExact.description:
```glsl
float sdOctahedronExact(vec3 p, float s) — exact, CHEAP
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/octahedron.glsl`

sdCone.description:
```glsl
float sdCone(vec3 p, float h, float r) — apex at +Y h, base radius r at y=0
```

Cone standing on the XZ plane with apex pointing up. PERF: CHEAP (3 clamps + sqrt). For a rounded-cap cone, see `sdRoundCone`.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = sdCone(p, 1.0, 0.5);  // 1 tall, 0.5 base radius
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/cone.glsl`

sdRoundCone.description:
```glsl
float sdRoundCone(vec3 p, float r1, float r2, float h)
```

Cone with spherical endcaps. r1 = base sphere radius, r2 = top sphere radius, h = distance between centers. Smooth tangent between caps. PERF: CHEAP.
When r1==r2 it becomes a Y-axis capsule; when r2==0 it becomes a pointed bullet.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = sdRoundCone(p, 0.3, 0.1, 1.0);  // tear-drop
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/round_cone.glsl`

sdPyramid.description:
```glsl
float sdPyramid(vec3 p, float h) — 1×1 base, apex at +Y h
```

Square-base pyramid. Base side 1 at y=0, apex at (0,h,0). PERF: MODERATE (heaviest of the new prims — multiple folds + sqrt). For arbitrary base size, scale the input: `sdPyramid(p/s, h)*s`.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = sdPyramid(p, 1.5);
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/pyramid.glsl`

sdEllipsoid.description:
```glsl
float sdEllipsoid(vec3 p, vec3 r)
```

Per-axis-scaled sphere. PERF: CHEAP (~2x sdSphere).
**Approximate** — only true distance when r is uniform. For highly anisotropic shapes (e.g. r = vec3(1.0, 0.1, 1.0)) raymarching may overshoot; reduce step size or multiply the result by ~0.5 to be safe.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = sdEllipsoid(p, vec3(0.5, 0.3, 0.2));  // flattened disk
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/ellipsoid.glsl`

sdHexPrism.description:
```glsl
float sdHexPrism(vec3 p, vec2 h) — h.x=apothem, h.y=half-depth
```

Hexagonal prism along Z axis. Like a pencil rod lying flat. PERF: FREE — no transcendentals.
h.x = flat-to-flat half-width (apothem of the hex), h.y = half-depth.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = sdHexPrism(p, vec2(0.3, 0.6));
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/hex_prism.glsl`

sdCappedTorus.description:
```glsl
float sdCappedTorus(vec3 p, vec2 sc, float ra, float rb)
```

Partial torus — only the slice within ±cap-angle from +Y is rendered. `sc = vec2(sin(cap), cos(cap))` where `cap` is the half-cap angle. `ra` = ring radius, `rb` = tube radius. PERF: CHEAP.

**Example:**
```glsl
float cap = 1.5707963;  // π/2 → top half
float d = sdCappedTorus(p, vec2(sin(cap), cos(cap)), 0.5, 0.08);
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/3d/capped_torus.glsl`

```glsl
float opSubtract(float a, float b) — max(a, -b)
```

```glsl
float opSmoothUnion(float a, float b, float k)
```

opRound.description:
```glsl
float opRound(float d, float r) — rounds any SDF
```

Rounds the silhouette of any SDF by `r`. PERF: FREE — one subtract. For boxes prefer the dedicated `sdRoundedBox` which folds the round into the same op.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = opRound(sdBox(p, vec3(1.0)), 0.1);
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/ops/round.glsl`

opOnion.description:
```glsl
float opOnion(float d, float t) — hollow shell of thickness 2t
```

Makes a hollow shell of thickness 2t from any solid SDF. Stack for concentric layers. PERF: FREE — abs + subtract.

Note: slightly breaks Lipschitz=1 continuity at the inner surface; use smaller raymarch steps inside thin shells.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = opOnion(sdSphere(p, 0.5), 0.05);  // 5cm-thick spherical shell
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/ops/onion.glsl`

opRepeat.description:
```glsl
vec3 opRepeat(vec3 p, vec3 c) — infinite tile
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/modifiers/repeat.glsl`

opTwist.description:
```glsl
vec3 opTwist(vec3 p, float k) — twist around Y
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/modifiers/twist.glsl`

opBend.description:
```glsl
vec3 opBend(vec3 p, float k) — bend around X
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/modifiers/bend.glsl`

opElongate.description:
```glsl
vec2|vec3 opElongate(vec p, vec h) — stretch a shape along axes
```

Inigo Quilez's elongation trick. Pass the result back into any SDF to stretch it.
PERF: FREE — clamp + subtract.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = sdSphere(opElongate(p, vec3(0.0, 0.5, 0.0)), 0.3);  // vertical capsule
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/modifiers/elongate.glsl`

opExtrude.description:
```glsl
float opExtrude(float d2d, float pz, float h) — extrude 2D SDF along Z
```

Turns any 2D SDF into a 3D prism. Pre-compute the 2D distance, then combine with a z-slab of half-height `h`. The standard iquilezles pattern — every 3D logo / glyph / "2D stamped on 3D" piece uses this.
PERF: FREE — abs + max + length on vec2.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d2 = sdCircle(p.xy, 0.3);
float d3 = opExtrude(d2, p.z, 0.5);  // circular pillar, half-height 0.5
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/modifiers/extrusion.glsl`

opRevolve.description:
```glsl
vec2 opRevolve(vec3 p, float offset) — solid of revolution around Y
```

Rotates a 2D profile around the Y axis. Returns a vec2 to feed any 2D SDF. `offset` shifts the rotation axis outward (offset=0 → disk-from-center profile; offset=ringR → torus-like).
Used for chair legs, wine glasses, vases, columns, rocket nozzles, bottle shapes — anything lathe-turned.
PERF: FREE — one 2D length.

**Example:**
```glsl
vec2 q = opRevolve(p, 0.0);
float d = sdCircle(q - vec2(0.0, 1.0), 0.3);  // sphere from revolved profile
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/modifiers/revolution.glsl`

pModPolar.description:
```glsl
float pModPolar(inout vec2 p, float repetitions)
```

*Auto-imports* `/sdf/modifiers/pmod_polar.glsl`

pModInterval1D.description:

```glsl
float pModInterval1D(inout float p, size, start, stop)
```

lookAt.description:
```glsl
mat3 lookAt(vec3 ro, vec3 target, float roll)
```

randomDisk.description:
```glsl
vec2 randomDisk(vec2 seed) — uniform disk sample
```

raymarch.description:
```glsl
float raymarch(ro, rd, tMax, maxSteps) — needs map()
```

*Auto-imports* `/include/raymarch.glsl`

pbrShade.description:
```glsl
vec3 pbrShade(albedo, rough, metal, N, V, L, lightColor)
```

*Auto-imports* `/include/raymarch.glsl`

softShadow.description:
```glsl
float softShadow(ro, rd, tMin, tMax, float k | int maxSteps | float k, int maxSteps)
```

Sphere-traced soft shadow. Three overloads:
- 5-arg with **float k** — penumbra sharpness (typical 4–32), 64 fixed steps.
- 5-arg with **int maxSteps** — k=8.0, variable step budget (use RM_SHADOW_STEPS_LOW/MID/HIGH).
- 6-arg `(k, maxSteps)` — explicit control over both.

Returns [0, 1] where 0 = full shadow, 1 = fully lit.
Needs a `map(vec3)` to be defined.

**Example:**
```glsl
float sh = softShadow(p + n * 0.01, L, 0.01, 10.0, RM_SHADOW_STEPS_MID);
```

*Auto-imports* `/include/raymarch.glsl`

applyFogLinear.description:
```glsl
vec3 applyFogLinear(col, dist, fogColor, near, far)
```

*Auto-imports* `/include/raymarch.glsl`

simpleIBL.description:
```glsl
vec3 simpleIBL(albedo, rough, metal, N, iblColor)
```

*Auto-imports* `/include/raymarch.glsl`

aaCoverage.description:
```glsl
float aaCoverage(float d [, float w]) — antialias an SDF edge
```

Screen-space SDF anti-aliasing using fwidth derivatives. Returns coverage in [0,1] — 1 inside, 0 outside, smooth boundary 1 pixel wide.

Use 2-arg form `aaCoverage(d, w)` when there are no screen-space derivatives (e.g. raymarch hit-tests; pass world-space width).
PERF: CHEAP — 1 fwidth + 1 smoothstep.

**Example:**
```glsl
float d = sdCircle(uv, 0.3);
float a = aaCoverage(d);
vec3 col = mix(bg, fg, a);
```

*Auto-imports* `/include/raymarch.glsl`

traceReflection.description:
```glsl
float traceReflection(hitP, normal, incident, tMax, maxSteps) — needs map()
```

Trace one reflected ray from a hit point. Returns the hit distance on the reflected ray, or -1 if it escapes (sample the sky in that case).
Combine with `fresnelSchlick` for realistic mirror-vs-grazing reflectivity.
PERF: MODERATE — one raymarch per call. Use RM_STEPS_LOW or RM_STEPS_MID — full step counts are wasted on reflections.

**Example:**
```glsl
float t2 = traceReflection(p, n, rd, 20.0, RM_STEPS_LOW);
vec3 reflCol = (t2 > 0.0) ? shadeAt(p + reflect(rd, n) * t2) : sky(reflect(rd, n));
```

*Auto-imports* `/include/raymarch.glsl`

fresnelSchlick.description:
```glsl
float|vec3 fresnelSchlick(float cosTheta, float|vec3 f0)
```

Schlick's Fresnel approximation. Returns reflection coefficient as a function of incident angle.
`cosTheta` = dot(view, normal) clamped to [0,1].
`f0` = base reflectance at normal incidence — 0.04 for plastic/dielectrics, the metallic albedo for metals.

There's also a `F_Schlick(HdotV, vec3 F0)` in render/lighting/pbr.glsl with the same math, designed for PBR call-sites.
PERF: FREE — one integer-exponent pow expansion.

*Auto-imports* `/include/raymarch.glsl`

portraitLight.description:
```glsl
vec3 portraitLight(n, v, l, baseCol, lightCol, intensity, shininess)
```

Single-light contribution for portrait shading — Blinn-Phong diffuse + specular for ONE light direction.
Use when you want to roll your own multi-light setup. For a complete drop-in shade pipeline, call `portraitShade` instead.
PERF: CHEAP — one diffuse dot + one blinnPhong.

*Auto-imports* `/render/portrait.glsl`

portraitShade.description:
```glsl
vec3 portraitShade(p, n, v, baseCol, shininess, sssAmount)
```

One-call portrait/character shading: **three-point lighting** (key + fill + rim) + **soft shadow** on the key + **AO** + **ambient** + **fake SSS** for skin.

Replaces ~30 lines of manual lighting with a single call. All constants (light dirs/colors/intensities, AO strength, SSS color) live at the top of `/render/portrait.glsl` and are tweakable — change in one place, re-light everywhere.

Args:
- `p` = world hit point
- `n` = surface normal
- `v` = view direction toward eye (= `-rd`)
- `baseCol` = material albedo
- `shininess` = Blinn-Phong exponent (use `PORTRAIT_SHIN_SKIN/HAIR/EYE`)
- `sssAmount` = 0..1 fake subsurface strength — set 0 for non-skin

Returns linear color — tonemap before output (use `tonemapACES`).
PERF: MODERATE — internally one softShadow + one ambientOcclusion (both raymarch loops).

**Example:**
```glsl
float matId = mapWithMat(p).y;
vec3 base; float shin; float sss;
if (matId < 1.5) { base = vec3(0.95,0.80,0.55); shin = PORTRAIT_SHIN_SKIN; sss = 1.0; }
else if (matId < 2.5) { base = vec3(0.15,0.10,0.08); shin = PORTRAIT_SHIN_HAIR; sss = 0.0; }
else { base = vec3(0.90); shin = PORTRAIT_SHIN_EYE; sss = 0.0; }
vec3 col = portraitShade(p, n, -rd, base, shin, sss);
col = tonemapACES(col);
```

*Auto-imports* `/render/portrait.glsl`

defaultMaterial.description:
```glsl
Material defaultMaterial()
```

Returns a neutral Material — black albedo, +Z normal, height=0, roughness=1, ao=1. Use as a starting point when you want to override only a few fields.

**Example:**
```glsl
Material m = defaultMaterial();
m.albedo = vec3(0.8, 0.2, 0.1);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/types.glsl`

brickMaterial.description:
```glsl
Material brickMaterial(vec2 st, vec2 id, float box, vec3 tint)
```

Brick-vs-mortar Material from a tile's SDF distance. Coord-system-agnostic — works for cartesian (running-bond) and polar (voussoir) tilings alike, because the normal comes from screen-space `dFdx`/`dFdy` on the SDF distance.

Args:
- `st` — full-resolution coords (for edge-noise sampling)
- `id` — brick identifier (for per-brick color jitter)
- `box` — brick SDF distance (negative inside the brick)
- `tint` — base brick color (use a BRICK_COLOR_* constant or your own)

The returned Material has: brick color jittered per-id with mortar in the gaps, screen-space normal, height=brick mask, roughness 0.3 in mortar / 0.85 on brick, and AO darkening at edges.

**Example:**
```glsl
vec2 id;
vec2 tile = brickTilePattern_runningBond(st, scale, id);
float box = brickSDF(tile);
Material m = brickMaterial(st, id, box, BRICK_COLOR_RED_CLAY);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/brick.glsl`

brickWeathered.description:
```glsl
Material brickWeathered(vec2 st, vec2 id, float box, vec3 tint)
```

Aged-brick variant of `brickMaterial`. Same call signature. Adds an FBM age-wash on color, moss in low-height regions (mortar lines + bottoms of bricks), hairline cracks from turbulence, and boosted AO + roughness for a non-pristine surface.

Use for old castle walls, abandoned buildings, sunken ruins. Drop-in swap with `brickMaterial`.

**Example:**
```glsl
Material m = brickWeathered(st, id, box, BRICK_COLOR_RED_CLAY);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/weathered.glsl`

brickPainted.description:
```glsl
Material brickPainted(vec2 st, vec2 id, float box, vec3 baseTint, vec3 paintCol)
```

Painted-brick variant. Flat paint layer over the brick, with random chip patches biased to brick rims that reveal `baseTint` underneath. Paint is glossier than raw brick — roughness drops where paint is present.

Args add `paintCol` to the regular brick set. Use BRICK_COLOR_PAINT_WHITE / _TEAL / _OXBLOOD or pass your own.

**Example:**
```glsl
Material m = brickPainted(st, id, box, BRICK_COLOR_RED_CLAY, BRICK_COLOR_PAINT_TEAL);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/painted.glsl`

brickConfigDefault.description:
```glsl
BrickConfig brickConfigDefault()
```

Returns a BrickConfig pre-populated with library defaults (pulled from /material/brick/constants.glsl). Use as a starting point — override only the fields you want, pass the result to any brick*MaterialEx variant.

Fields:
- **Edge noise**: `noiseScale`, `noiseAmount`, `noiseBand`
- **Color**: `colorJitter`, `mortarColor`
- **Roughness mapping**: `roughnessMortar`, `roughnessBrick`
- **Baked AO**: `aoStrength`, `aoBand`
- **Weathered only**: `ageScale`, `ageStrength`, `mossScale`, `mossAmount`, `mossColor`, `crackScale`, `crackAmount`
- **Painted only**: `chipScale`, `chipThreshold`, `paintNoiseScale`, `paintRoughness`

**Example:**
```glsl
BrickConfig cfg = brickConfigDefault();
cfg.colorJitter = 0.6;
cfg.mortarColor = vec3(0.3);
Material m = brickMaterialEx(st, id, box, BRICK_COLOR_RED_CLAY, cfg);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/config.glsl`

brickMaterialEx.description:
```glsl
Material brickMaterialEx(vec2 st, vec2 id, float box, vec3 tint, BrickConfig cfg)
```

Full-control variant of brickMaterial. Same shape as the shortcut but takes a BrickConfig to override edge-noise / color jitter / mortar color / roughness mapping / AO per call.

See `brickConfigDefault` for the full field list. Use the shortcut `brickMaterial(...)` if you don't need overrides.

**Example:**
```glsl
BrickConfig cfg = brickConfigDefault();
cfg.colorJitter = 0.6;
Material m = brickMaterialEx(st, id, box, BRICK_COLOR_RED_CLAY, cfg);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/brick.glsl`

brickWeatheredEx.description:
```glsl
Material brickWeatheredEx(vec2 st, vec2 id, float box, vec3 tint, BrickConfig cfg)
```

Full-control weathered brick. Consumes the weathered-only fields of BrickConfig: `ageScale`, `ageStrength`, `mossScale`, `mossAmount`, `mossColor`, `crackScale`, `crackAmount`.

**Example:**
```glsl
BrickConfig cfg = brickConfigDefault();
cfg.mossAmount = 0.9;
cfg.mossColor = vec3(0.15, 0.30, 0.10); // wet moss
Material m = brickWeatheredEx(st, id, box, BRICK_COLOR_RED_CLAY, cfg);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/weathered.glsl`

brickPaintedEx.description:
```glsl
Material brickPaintedEx(vec2 st, vec2 id, float box, vec3 baseTint, vec3 paintCol, BrickConfig cfg)
```

Full-control painted brick. Consumes the painted-only fields of BrickConfig: `chipScale`, `chipThreshold`, `paintNoiseScale`, `paintRoughness`.

**Example:**
```glsl
BrickConfig cfg = brickConfigDefault();
cfg.chipThreshold = 0.6;  // more chipping
Material m = brickPaintedEx(st, id, box, BRICK_COLOR_RED_CLAY, BRICK_COLOR_PAINT_TEAL, cfg);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/painted.glsl`

brickTile.description:
```glsl
vec2 brickTile(vec2 st, float zoom, float rowAmount, out vec2 id)
```

Generic brick-tile layout function. `rowAmount` = 1 for stack bond, 2 for running bond, 3 for stretcher bond. Prefer the named `brickTilePattern_*` wrappers for readability.

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/pattern.glsl`

brickTilePattern_runningBond.description:
```glsl
vec2 brickTilePattern_runningBond(vec2 st, float zoom, out vec2 id)
```

Standard running-bond layout — every other row offset by half a brick. The default brick look.

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/pattern.glsl`

brickTilePattern_stack.description:
```glsl
vec2 brickTilePattern_stack(vec2 st, float zoom, out vec2 id)
```

Stack-bond layout — bricks line up in a rigid grid with no row offset. Modern / industrial look.

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/pattern.glsl`

brickTilePattern_stretcher.description:
```glsl
vec2 brickTilePattern_stretcher(vec2 st, float zoom, out vec2 id)
```

Stretcher-bond layout — every third row offset by one-third. Busier than running bond.

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/pattern.glsl`

brickTilePattern_basket.description:
```glsl
vec2 brickTilePattern_basket(vec2 st, float zoom, out vec2 id)
```

Basket-weave layout — 2x1 pairs alternating horizontal/vertical in a checkerboard. Common in floors and courtyards.

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/pattern.glsl`

brickTilePattern_herringbone.description:
```glsl
vec2 brickTilePattern_herringbone(vec2 st, float zoom, out vec2 id)
```

Simplified herringbone — rows alternate horizontal/vertical brick orientation. Not a true 45°-rotated herringbone; useful as a starting point you can extend.

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/pattern.glsl`

brickSDF.description:
```glsl
float brickSDF(vec2 tile)
```

Standard brick shape SDF in tile-local space. Uses BRICK_HALF_EXTENTS and BRICK_CORNER_RADIUS from constants. Feed it the return value of any `brickTilePattern_*` to get the per-pixel SDF distance.

**Example:**
```glsl
vec2 id; vec2 tile = brickTilePattern_runningBond(st, 5.0, id);
float box = brickSDF(tile);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/pattern.glsl`

brickBevelNormal.description:
```glsl
vec3 brickBevelNormal(vec2 tile, float bevel, float depth)
```

High-quality analytical bevel normal for cartesian brick tilings. Samples the brick SDF at 4 neighbor positions, builds a smooth height field, returns the gradient → normal.

Use it to OVERRIDE the dFdx/dFdy normal that `brickMaterial` produces by default — better quality, resolution-independent, no aliasing at edges. Cost: 4 extra `sdRoundedBox` calls.

Does NOT work for polar tilings (voussoirs) — use dFdx in those cases.

**Example:**
```glsl
Material m = brickMaterial(st, id, box, BRICK_COLOR_RED_CLAY);
m.normal = brickBevelNormal(tile, BRICK_BEVEL, BRICK_DEPTH); // override
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/brick/pattern.glsl`

skyMaterial.description:
```glsl
Material skyMaterial(vec2 st)
```

Vertical-gradient sky Material — warm horizon to cool zenith. `height = 0` so `shadeMaterial` keeps it flat (no spec, no diffuse falloff).

**Example:**
```glsl
if (isOpening) mat = skyMaterial(st);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/sky/sky.glsl`

shadeMaterial.description:
```glsl
vec3 shadeMaterial(Material m, vec3 lightDir, vec2 st)
```

Blinn-Phong-ish shading consuming a Material. Designed for 2D pieces (screen-space normals, fixed view direction); for 3D raymarched scenes use `portraitShade` or roll your own using `lambert`/`blinnPhong` from /render/lighting/.

`m.height` gates diffuse/spec — sky materials (height=0) stay perfectly flat. `m.roughness` controls spec exponent: 0 → tight mirror, 1 → broad matte.

**Example:**
```glsl
vec3 lightDir = normalize(vec3(-0.5, 0.7, 1.0));
vec3 col = shadeMaterial(mat, lightDir, st);
```

*Auto-imports* `/material/shade.glsl`
